title: Version Controlled SQL Database Dolt Releases 2.0 with Automatic Storage Cleanup and Compression
gist: DoltHub's Dolt 2.0 adds default-on garbage collection and a new 'archives' compression format cutting storage 30-50%, claims to have flipped from being 10-20x slower than MySQL to now beating it on sysbench, and adds beta version-controlled vector indexes, positioning Git-style data versioning as an emerging standard against rivals like LakeFS and Nessie.

insights.claim: Dolt's copy-on-write storage model means every intermediate committed transaction state is preserved to disk, so operations like bulk imports generate large volumes of disk garbage that previously had to be manually managed.
insights.speaker: Renato Losio
insights.so_what: Explains why storage costs balloon for teams doing frequent bulk imports into Dolt, and why the new automated GC is a meaningful operational fix rather than a cosmetic feature.
insights.quote: Dolt is copy-on-write so all intermediate committed transaction state is preserved to disk.
insights.locator: Tim Sehn, founder and CEO of DoltHub, explains why garbage collection matters

insights.claim: Dolt 2.0 enables automatic garbage collection and archive compression by default, removing the need for operators to manually trigger cleanup of accumulated disk garbage.
insights.speaker: Renato Losio
insights.so_what: Reduces operational burden and unplanned disk-cost growth for teams running Dolt at scale, a common adoption blocker for version-controlled databases.
insights.locator: Version 2.0 adds automated garbage collection and archive compression enabled by default

insights.claim: Dolt 2.0 introduces a new on-disk 'archives' format that uses dictionary compression to deduplicate storage, cutting Dolt's storage footprint by an estimated 30-50%.
insights.speaker: Renato Losio
insights.so_what: A 30-50% storage reduction directly lowers the cost of running Dolt for large or long-history datasets, improving its economics versus non-versioned databases.
insights.quote: reduce the storage footprint by 30-50% through dictionary compression to deduplicate storage
insights.locator: The database adds a new on-disk format called "archives"

insights.claim: Dolt stores data in content-addressed Prolly Trees, which gives it row-level versioning, efficient structural sharing across versions, and fast diffs and merges without full data duplication.
insights.speaker: Renato Losio
insights.so_what: The Prolly Tree structure is the core mechanism enabling Dolt's Git-like semantics at the row level, distinguishing its approach from lakehouse-level versioning tools.
insights.locator: It stores data in content-addressed Prolly Trees

insights.claim: DoltHub's own sysbench benchmarking shows Dolt evolved from roughly 10x slower than MySQL on reads and 20x slower on writes to now being 13% faster than MySQL on writes and 5% faster on reads.
insights.speaker: Renato Losio
insights.so_what: If independently verified, this closes the historical performance gap that kept version-controlled SQL databases confined to niche use cases, making Dolt a plausible option for latency-sensitive production workloads.
insights.quote: We started at about 10X slower on reads and 20X slower on writes than MySQL. We’ve worked tirelessly to improve Dolt’s performance and we are now 13% faster than MySQL on writes and 5% faster on reads.
insights.locator: Discussing how the team used sysbench to measure and benchmark the latency

insights.claim: Dolt 2.0 adds beta support for version-controlled vector indexes built on MariaDB's Vector type, with DoltHub claiming Dolt is the only database that version-controls vectors.
insights.speaker: Renato Losio
insights.so_what: If the claim holds, Dolt could carve out a differentiated niche for AI/ML teams needing auditable, rollback-capable vector data alongside their embeddings pipeline.
insights.quote: Dolt is the only database that version-controls vectors
insights.locator: Dolt 2.0 also introduces beta support for version-controlled vector indexes

insights.claim: DoltgreSQL, the Postgres-compatible version of Dolt that shares the same storage engine and version-control interfaces as the MySQL-compatible Dolt, remains in beta while Dolt itself is generally available.
insights.speaker: Renato Losio
insights.so_what: Teams wanting Postgres compatibility with Dolt's versioning still face beta-stage risk, while MySQL-compatible workloads get the mature, GA path first.
insights.locator: DoltgreSQL, the Postgres-compatible version of Dolt

insights.claim: Git-like data versioning is a competitive category with multiple different technical approaches: Dolt implements it as a full MySQL-compatible database, LakeFS applies it to data lakes without duplicating large datasets, and Nessie provides a Git-like transactional catalog for data lakes.
insights.speaker: Renato Losio
insights.so_what: Investors and architects evaluating data-versioning tooling should map use case to approach: full SQL database (Dolt) vs. lake-level catalog/versioning (LakeFS, Nessie) rather than treating the category as one homogeneous market.
insights.locator: Dolt is not the only Git-like tool for data versioning

insights.claim: Data engineering writer Simon Späti argues that Git-like workflows for data are becoming table stakes, eventually delivering better change management, testing on production data, fast rollbacks, isolated experiments, and safer deployments.
insights.speaker: Renato Losio
insights.so_what: Signals a broader industry trend toward treating data like code, which would expand the addressable market for Dolt, LakeFS, Nessie and similar Git-inspired data tools over time rather than immediately.
insights.quote: Git-like workflows are becoming table stakes.
insights.locator: Simon Späti wrote a series of articles explaining what a Git-inspired approach to data is
